title: The Cost of Many Sequential Steps
concept: Each of the ~1000 denoising steps requires a full forward pass through the network, and steps must happen one after another, not in parallel.

exercises.prompt: If you had to bake 1000 tiny cupcakes one at a time in a single oven that fits only one cupcake, what's the big problem?

options.text: It would take a really long time since each one must finish before the next starts
There is no problem at all

exercises.explain: Exactly! Doing things one after another (sequentially) instead of all at once (in parallel) takes a long time when there are many steps.

cards.title: Why Is AI Image Generation Sometimes Slow?
cards.body: Some AI art generators need to think in about 1000 tiny steps to turn fuzzy noise into a clear picture. Each step depends on the one before it. Why can't the computer just do all 1000 steps at once and finish instantly?

cards.title: Sequential Denoising Steps
cards.body: A diffusion model cleans up a noisy image bit by bit, over about 1000 steps. Each step needs a full pass through the neural network, and each step needs the result from the step right before it. Because of this, the steps must happen one after another (sequentially), not all at the same time (in parallel).

cards.title: Climbing a Staircase
cards.body: Imagine a staircase with 1000 steps, and you can only climb one step at a time using the step below as support. You can't skip ahead to step 500 without passing through the steps before it. Denoising works the same way — each step relies on the previous one, so it takes time to reach the top.

cards.title: Turning Noise into a Cat Picture
cards.body: Step 1: The image is pure random static, like TV snow. Step 2: The network removes a tiny bit of noise, revealing a hint of shape. ...This repeats about 1000 times, each pass through the network, until step 1000 shows a clear, sharp picture of a cat. Each step must wait for the one before it to finish.

cards.title: Myth: More Computers Fixes Everything
cards.body: You might think adding more powerful computers or more chips would let all 1000 steps happen at once, making it instant. But that's not true! Since each step needs the output of the previous step, you can't skip or parallelize them, no matter how many extra chips you throw at the problem. The steps are stuck in a line, waiting their turn.

cards.title: Parallel Work vs Sequential Work
cards.body: Some computer tasks can be split up and done all at once, but denoising can't — it's a strict chain of steps.

left.text: Coloring 100 separate coloring pages — many people can color different pages at the exact same time

right.text: Passing a note down a line of 1000 people, one at a time — person 500 can't get the note until person 499 passes it along

exercises.prompt: Why do the ~1000 denoising steps in a diffusion model take a while to complete?

options.text: Because each step needs the result of the previous step, so they must happen one after another
Because the computer forgets what it was doing
Because the image file is too large to open
Because the internet connection is slow

exercises.explain: Each denoising step depends on the previous step's output, so they must run sequentially, one full network pass at a time.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You can speed up diffusion denoising by running all 1000 steps at the exact same time on different chips.
exercises.explain: False! Each step depends on the output of the one before it, so they cannot be run in parallel — they must happen in order.

exercises.sentence: Denoising steps must happen one after another because each step needs the ___ of the step before it.
exercises.explain: Each denoising step uses the result (output) from the previous step as its starting point, creating a chain that can't be broken apart.

exercises.prompt: Which everyday activity is most like the sequential nature of denoising steps?

options.text: Passing a whispered message down a line of people, one at a time
Many people painting on their own separate canvases at once
A group of friends texting each other independently at the same time

exercises.explain: Passing a message down a line matches denoising perfectly — each person (step) must wait to receive the message from the person before them before passing it on.

exercises.prompt: A friend says: 'If we just buy a supercomputer with a million chips, diffusion image generation with 1000 steps will finish instantly, because all chips can work at once.' What's wrong with this claim?

options.text: The steps are sequential — each one depends on the previous step's output, so extra chips can't make them run at the same time
Nothing is wrong, more chips always means instant results for any task
Supercomputers cannot generate images at all
The claim is correct because 1000 is a small number

exercises.explain: The key issue is that denoising steps are chained together — step 500 needs the output of step 499. Because of this dependency, no amount of extra computing chips can make the steps happen simultaneously; they must still occur in order, one full pass at a time.
